---
title: "Designing a dark-first theme"
description: "Why start from dark, how to structure your tokens and avoid contrast pitfalls."
pubDate: 2026-07-08
tags: ["design", "css", "accessibility"]
draft: false
---

Designing dark first flips the usual process: dark mode is the baseline, light mode is the variant.

## Semantic tokens, not raw colors

```css
.dark {
  --background: oklch(0 0 0);
  --foreground: oklch(0.984 0.003 248);
  --accent: oklch(0.723 0.219 149.6);
}
```

A component never references a hex value: it consumes `--background`, `--accent`, `--muted-foreground`. The entire theme switches by toggling one class on `<html>`.

## The inverted contrast trap

A gray that reads fine on white becomes illegible on a black background. Both modes must be tested separately, aiming for 4.5:1 on body text.

## The no-flash script

```html
<script>
  const stored = localStorage.getItem("theme");
  const theme = stored ?? (matchMedia("(prefers-color-scheme: light)").matches ? "light" : "dark");
  document.documentElement.classList.toggle("dark", theme === "dark");
</script>
```

Placed before the first paint, it eliminates the flash of incorrect theme on load.

## Desaturate, don't invert

In dark mode, saturated colors vibrate. The right move is to raise the lightness and lower the chroma: a `green-600` in light becomes a `green-500` in dark, never the other way around.
